title: Graphs of $y = |f(x)|$ where $f(x)$ is Linear - Easy

questions.question: Given the linear function $f(x)=x+6$, state the coordinates of the vertex of the graph $y=|f(x)|$.
questions.explanation:
The vertex occurs where $f(x)=0$, so $x+6=0$ gives $x=-6$. At the vertex, $y=|f(x)|=0$, giving $(-6,0)$.

**Common mistake:** Misidentifying the sign of the x-coordinate, picking 6 instead of -6.

questions.question: Given $f(x)=3x-12$, find the y-intercept of the graph $y=|f(x)|$.
questions.explanation:
Substitute $x=0$: $|f(0)|=|3(0)-12|=|-12|=12$.

**Common mistake:** Identifying the x-intercept of $f(x)$ (4) as the y-intercept or failing to take the modulus and giving -12.

questions.question: When sketching $y=|f(x)|$ from a linear graph $y=f(x)$, what happens to any part of the line below the $x$-axis?
options: It is removed from the graph
questions.explanation:
Taking the modulus makes all $y$-values non-negative, so negative sections of $y=f(x)$ are reflected upward.

**Common mistake:** Thinking the negative section is deleted rather than reflected.

questions.question: Which statement is always true for the range of $y=|f(x)|$, where $f$ is any function?
questions.explanation:
A modulus output is never negative, so the range of $y=|f(x)|$ always satisfies $y \ge 0$.

**Common mistake:** Confusing $y \ge 0$ with $y>0$ — the graph can touch the $x$-axis at the vertex.

questions.question: Given $f(x)=2x-10$, identify the coordinates of the minimum point on the graph $y=|f(x)|+3$.
questions.explanation:
$|f(x)|$ is minimised when $f(x)=0$, so $2x-10=0$ gives $x=5$. Then $y=|0|+3=3$, giving the point $(5,3)$.

**Common mistake:** Neglecting the vertical shift (+3) or failing to solve $f(x)=0$ correctly.
